title: ONGC Physics — Advanced Mock 15 — Final Challenge
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The fundamental group pi_1 of a circle S^1 is isomorphic to:
questions.solution: The fundamental group of S^1 is pi_1(S^1) = Z (the integers), where the integer n represents the winding number of loops around the circle. This is computed via the covering space R -> S^1 given by t -> exp(2*pi*i*t).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The spectrum of the Hamiltonian for a free particle on a circle (periodic boundary conditions) consists of:
options: A continuous spectrum from 0 to infinity
questions.solution: For a particle on a ring of circumference L with periodic boundary conditions psi(x+L) = psi(x), the wavefunctions are psi_n = exp(2*pi*i*n*x/L) with energies E_n = (2*pi*n*hbar)^2/(2*m*L^2). The spectrum is discrete because of the compact domain.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In classical mechanics, gauge invariance of the charged particle Lagrangian under A -> A + grad(chi) requires the transformation psi -> psi * exp(ie*chi/hbar). This is an example of:
questions.solution: Local (spacetime-dependent) U(1) gauge symmetry requires the introduction of the gauge field A_mu to maintain invariance. This is the classical analogue of the minimal coupling prescription in quantum mechanics and is the foundation of electromagnetism as a gauge theory.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED), an atom in a high-finesse cavity can exhibit vacuum Rabi oscillations. The frequency of these oscillations is:
questions.solution: On resonance (Delta = 0), the vacuum Rabi frequency is 2g where g is the single-photon coupling strength. The atom and cavity exchange energy at this frequency. For strong coupling (g >> kappa, Gamma), coherent quantum dynamics dominate. This is the basis of circuit QED and cavity QED experiments.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Bell's inequality shows that quantum mechanics is incompatible with local hidden variable theories. The maximum quantum violation (Tsirelson bound) for the CHSH inequality is:
questions.solution: Local hidden variables: |S| <= 2 (Bell/CHSH inequality). Quantum mechanics: |S| <= 2*sqrt(2) ~ 2.828 (Tsirelson bound). This maximum is achieved by maximally entangled states and specific measurement settings. The experimental violation confirms quantum nonlocality.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Thermodynamics & Statistical Physics · Black Hole Thermodynamics
questions.stem: Hawking radiation from a black hole has a temperature T_H = hbar*c^3/(8*pi*G*M*k_B). For a solar mass black hole, this temperature is approximately:
questions.solution: T_H ~ 60 nK for M = M_sun. This is far below the CMB temperature (2.7 K), so stellar black holes absorb more radiation than they emit. Only primordial black holes with M << M_sun would have T_H > 2.7 K and could evaporate. The evaporation time scales as M^3.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The transition from hadronic matter to quark-gluon plasma (QGP) is predicted by lattice QCD to occur at a critical temperature of approximately:
questions.solution: Lattice QCD predicts the deconfinement transition at T_c ~ 155 MeV (1.8 x 10^12 K). At this temperature, hadrons 'melt' into a plasma of quarks and gluons. The crossover (not a sharp phase transition for physical quark masses) was confirmed at RHIC and LHC.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: CP violation in the Standard Model arises from the complex phase in the CKM matrix. The parameter epsilon in the kaon system measures:
options: The amount of CP violation in K^0-K^0_bar mixing
questions.solution: epsilon_K ~ 2.2 x 10^-3 parametrizes indirect CP violation in the neutral kaon system (K_L -> pi*pi decay). It arises from the imaginary part of box diagrams contributing to K^0-K^0_bar mixing. Direct CP violation is parametrized by epsilon'/epsilon.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The first direct detection of gravitational waves (GW150914) by LIGO was from:
options: Merging black holes (binary black hole merger)
questions.solution: GW150914 (September 14, 2015) detected gravitational waves from the merger of two black holes (36 and 29 solar masses) 1.3 billion light-years away. The signal matched general relativistic predictions perfectly. Nobel Prize in Physics 2017 (Weiss, Barish, Thorne).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A 3D topological insulator is characterized by:
options: Bulk band gap with conducting surface states protected by time-reversal symmetry
questions.solution: Topological insulators (Bi_2Se_3, Bi_2Te_3) have an insulating bulk but conducting surface states with Dirac cone dispersion. These states are topologically protected: they cannot be gapped by time-reversal symmetric perturbations. The Z_2 topological invariant distinguishes trivial and topological insulators.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The protein folding problem concerns how a polypeptide chain acquires its native 3D structure. The folding process occurs on a timescale of:
questions.solution: Most small proteins fold in microseconds to milliseconds (Levinthal's paradox: random search would take 10^77 years). The folding funnel landscape guides the process. AlphaFold (DeepMind, 2020) solved the protein structure prediction problem using deep learning.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Earth's magnetic field is generated by the geodynamo - convective motion of liquid iron in the outer core. The field reverses polarity irregularly. The last reversal (Brunhes-Matuyama) occurred approximately:
questions.solution: The Brunhes-Matuyama reversal occurred ~780 ka. The current field is weakening (~10% per century) and the magnetic north pole is moving from Canada toward Siberia. Some interpret this as a possible precursor to reversal, though the process is not periodic.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Climate sensitivity is defined as the equilibrium temperature change for a doubling of atmospheric CO_2. The IPCC AR6 best estimate is approximately:
questions.solution: IPCC AR6 (2021) gives equilibrium climate sensitivity as 2.5-4.0 degrees C (best estimate 3 degrees C). This ranges from 'likely' to 'very likely' given various feedback mechanisms (water vapor, clouds, albedo). This is a critical parameter for climate projections.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging), the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons at 1.5 T is approximately:
questions.solution: The Larmor frequency f = gamma*B/(2*pi) where gamma/2pi = 42.58 MHz/T for ^1H. At 1.5 T: f = 42.58 * 1.5 = 63.87 MHz. At 3 T: 127.7 MHz. Higher field strength gives better signal-to-noise ratio but increases specific absorption rate (SAR).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators on a Hilbert space states that the operator can be written as:
options: An infinite sum of lambda_n * |n><n| where lambda_n are eigenvalues
questions.solution: The spectral theorem: T = sum_n lambda_n P_n where P_n = |n><n> are projectors onto eigenspaces. This is fundamental for quantum mechanics (observable eigenvalues) and functional analysis. For compact operators the eigenvalues accumulate only at zero.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A rolling coin (without slipping) is an example of a non-holonomic system because:
options: The constraint cannot be integrated to reduce the number of generalized coordinates
questions.solution: Non-holonomic constraints are velocity-dependent and non-integrable (e.g., v = omega*r for rolling). They reduce the accessible velocity space but not the configuration space. The coin can reach any position/orientation despite the constraint - the configuration space is still full-dimensional.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The quantum Hall effect exhibits quantized Hall conductance sigma_xy = nu*e^2/h where nu is the filling factor. For the integer quantum Hall effect, nu equals:
questions.solution: The integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing, Nobel 1985): sigma_xy = n*e^2/h (n = 1, 2, 3...). The quantization is exact to 1 part in 10^9, independent of sample geometry. This is used as a resistance standard. The fractional quantum Hall effect (Laughlin, Nobel 1998) has nu = p/q.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In quantum electrodynamics (QED), the anomalous magnetic moment of the electron (g-2)/2 arises from:
options: Loop corrections (virtual electron-positron pairs and photons)
questions.solution: The Dirac equation predicts g = 2 exactly. QED corrections (Schwinger, 1948): (g-2)/2 = alpha/(2*pi) ~ 0.00116 (first order). Higher orders agree with experiment to 12 decimal places - the most precise prediction in physics.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The semi-empirical mass formula (Bethe-Weizsäcker) for nuclear binding energy includes volume, surface, Coulomb, asymmetry, and pairing terms. The pairing term accounts for:
options: Even-even nuclei being more bound than odd-A nuclei
The Pauli exclusion principle in nuclear matter
questions.solution: The pairing term Delta(A) = +a_p/A^(3/4) for even-even, 0 for odd-A, -a_p/A^(3/4) for odd-odd nuclei. It reflects the tendency of nucleons to form Cooper-like pairs (S=0, L=0), analogous to superconductivity in nuclei. Even-even nuclei have the highest binding energy per nucleon.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Lawson criterion for a self-sustaining D-T fusion reaction specifies the product of density and confinement time n*tau_E must exceed approximately:
questions.solution: The Lawson criterion: n*tau_E > 1.5 x 10^20 s/m^3 at T ~ 15 keV for D-T fusion. This defines the 'triple product' n*tau_E*T > 3 x 10^21 keV*s/m^3. ITER aims to achieve n*tau_E ~ 3 x 10^21 s/m^3, exceeding the criterion by a factor of 15.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A squeezed state of light reduces quantum noise in one quadrature below the vacuum level while increasing it in the orthogonal quadrature. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle is satisfied because:
options: The product of uncertainties Delta_x * Delta_p >= hbar/2
questions.solution: Squeezing redistributes quantum noise: Delta_x < sqrt(hbar/2) but Delta_p > sqrt(hbar/2) such that the product still satisfies the uncertainty principle. Applications: gravitational wave detection (LIGO), quantum communication, sub-shot-noise measurements.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Superconducting transmon qubits operate at temperatures of approximately:
questions.solution: Transmon qubits require dilution refrigerator temperatures (10-20 mK) to suppress thermal excitations (k_B*T << hbar*omega, where omega ~ 5 GHz). At 20 mK, thermal population is ~10^(-5). IBM and Google use transmons in their quantum processors.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Gutenberg-Richter law describes the frequency-magnitude distribution of earthquakes: log10(N) = a - b*M. The b-value is typically close to:
questions.solution: b ~ 1.0 globally, meaning for each magnitude 5 earthquake there are ~10 magnitude 4 and ~100 magnitude 3 earthquakes. Deviations from b=1 indicate stress conditions: b < 1 suggests increased stress (fewer small earthquakes), b > 1 suggests stress release.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Hodgkin-Huxley model describes action potential generation in neurons. The model includes voltage-gated ion channels for:
options: Sodium (Na^+) and Potassium (K^+) with a leak current
questions.solution: The HH model (Nobel Prize 1963): C_m*dV/dt = -g_Na*m^3*h*(V-E_Na) - g_K*n^4*(V-E_K) - g_L*(V-E_L). Four coupled ODEs for V, m, h, n. The sodium current is responsible for the upstroke, potassium for repolarization. This model revolutionized computational neuroscience.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Technetium-99m is the most widely used radioisotope in diagnostic nuclear medicine. Its half-life is approximately:
questions.solution: Tc-99m has t_1/2 = 6.01 hours, ideal for diagnostic imaging: long enough for imaging, short enough to limit patient dose. It emits 140 keV gamma rays (optimal for gamma cameras). Produced from Mo-99/Tc-99m generators.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Fokker-Planck equation describes the time evolution of the probability distribution for a stochastic process. For Brownian motion, it reduces to:
questions.solution: The Fokker-Planck equation: dp/dt = -d/dx(A*p) + (1/2)*d^2/dx^2(B^2*p). For Brownian motion (A=0, B=sqrt(2D)): dp/dt = D*d^2p/dx^2 (diffusion equation). This connects microscopic (Langevin equation) to macroscopic (probability distribution) descriptions.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Hamiltonian mechanics has a natural geometric structure on phase space given by the symplectic 2-form omega. Liouville's theorem (phase space volume preservation) follows from:
options: The closedness of the symplectic form (d*omega = 0)
questions.solution: The symplectic form omega = sum dp_i ^ dq_i is closed (d*omega = 0). By Cartan's formula, the Lie derivative L_X omega = d(i_X omega) + i_X(d*omega) = 0 for the Hamiltonian vector field X. This implies phase space volume is preserved under Hamiltonian flow.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) is based on the gauge group SU(3). The gauge bosons (gluons) carry:
options: Color charge (unlike photons which are electrically neutral)
questions.solution: Unlike photons (neutral under U(1)_em), gluons carry color charge (8 types, one for each SU(3) generator). This leads to gluon self-interaction, asymptotic freedom (alpha_s decreases at short distances), and confinement (color charge cannot be isolated).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The rotation curves of spiral galaxies show flat velocity profiles at large radii, implying the presence of:
questions.solution: Galaxy rotation curves: v(r) ~ constant for r >> optical disk, implying M(r) ~ r (enclosed mass grows linearly). This requires a dark matter halo extending well beyond the visible galaxy. The virial theorem and gravitational lensing provide independent confirmation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A Josephson junction consists of two superconductors separated by a thin barrier. The Josephson relations describe the supercurrent I = I_c*sin(phi) and the voltage-frequency relation V = (hbar/2e)*d*phi/dt. The second relation implies:
options: A DC voltage produces an AC current (AC Josephson effect)
An AC voltage produces a DC current
The current is proportional to resistance
questions.solution: The AC Josephson effect: a DC voltage V produces oscillations at frequency f = 2eV/h ~ 483.6 GHz/mV. This is used as a voltage standard (the Josephson constant 2e/h). The inverse: microwaves at frequency f produce quantized voltage steps V_n = n*h*f/(2e).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Grand Unified Theories (GUTs) predict that protons are not absolutely stable. The current experimental lower limit on the proton lifetime is approximately:
questions.solution: Super-Kamiokande limits: tau(p -> e^+*pi^0) > 2.4 x 10^34 years, tau(p -> K^+*nu) > 5.9 x 10^33 years. GUTs predict proton decay via X-boson exchange with tau ~ M_GUT^4/(alpha_GUT^2*m_p^5). Minimal SU(5) is ruled out, but supersymmetric GUTs are still viable.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Kolmogorov complexity K(x) of a string x measures the shortest program that produces x. For a truly random string of length n, K(x) is approximately:
questions.solution: A random string is incompressible: K(x) ~ n (the shortest description is the string itself). This connects algorithmic information theory to thermodynamics: entropy measures the average information content, analogous to Kolmogorov complexity for typical strings.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Nuclear isomers are metastable excited states of nuclei. The longest-lived isomer known is:
questions.solution: Hf-178m2 has t_1/2 = 31 years, storing 2.45 MeV of energy. Ta-180m has an even longer half-life (~10^15 years) but is technically a ground state isomer. The isomeric transition releases gamma rays, making isomers candidates for gamma-ray lasers.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) extract heat from hot dry rock at depths of typically:
questions.solution: EGS targets hot dry rock (HDR) at 3-10 km depth where temperatures reach 150-350 degrees C. Water is injected to fracture the rock and extract heat. The geothermal gradient averages ~25-30 degrees C/km. EGS could provide baseload renewable energy globally.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing relies on RNA-DNA hybridization for target recognition. The energy of binding between guide RNA and target DNA is typically:
questions.solution: RNA-DNA hybrid stability is ~2-3 kcal/mol per base pair (weaker than DNA-DNA ~3 kcal/mol). The Cas9 protein requires ~20 bp of complementarity for stable binding, with the PAM sequence (NGG) providing initial recognition. Mismatches near the PAM are less tolerated.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In radiation therapy, the linear-quadratic model describes cell survival as S = exp(-alpha*D - beta*D^2). The alpha/beta ratio determines:
questions.solution: The alpha/beta ratio (Gy) characterizes tissue response to fractionation: high alpha/beta (~10 Gy) for tumors, low alpha/beta (~3 Gy) for late-responding normal tissues. This is the basis for hypofractionation schedules in modern radiotherapy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In quantum field theory, renormalization removes infinities by absorbing them into redefined (renormalized) parameters. The number of independent renormalization constants in QED is:
questions.solution: QED requires renormalization of: (1) charge (vacuum polarization), (2) electron mass (self-energy), (3) field strength/wavefunction (vertex corrections + self-energy). The Ward identity ensures Z_1 = Z_2, reducing the number of independent constants.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The baryon asymmetry of the universe (more matter than antimatter) requires three Sakharov conditions. One of these is:
options: C and CP violation in particle interactions
questions.solution: Sakharov conditions (1967): (1) Baryon number violation, (2) C and CP violation, (3) Departure from thermal equilibrium. These explain why matter survived baryon-antibaryon annihilation in the early universe. CP violation is observed in K and B meson systems.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The kilonova observed during the neutron star merger GW170817 was powered by:
questions.solution: Kilonovae are powered by radioactive decay of heavy elements (r-process nucleosynthesis) synthesized in the neutron-rich ejecta. This confirmed that neutron star mergers are a major (possibly dominant) site of r-process element production, including gold, platinum, and uranium.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Neuromorphic computing chips (e.g., Intel Loihi, IBM TrueNorth) mimic the brain's architecture by using:
options: Spiking neural networks with event-driven computation
questions.solution: Neuromorphic chips use artificial spiking neurons and synapses with event-driven (asynchronous) computation. This enables low-power inference at the edge: Loihi 2 achieves 1000x energy efficiency vs GPUs for certain inference tasks. Applications: sensory processing, robotics, adaptive control.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A can do a work in 15 days and B in 20 days, together they can do it in:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A's salary is 20% more than B's, then B's salary is less than A's by:
questions.solution: Let B = 100, A = 120. B is less than A by 20/120 = 16.67%.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Rs. 5000 at 8% per annum simple interest for 3 years amounts to:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A and B invest Rs. 6000 and Rs. 8000 respectively. Profit of Rs. 2100 is to be shared in the ratio:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The sum of ages of 5 children born at 3-year intervals is 50. The age of the youngest child is:
questions.solution: Let youngest = x. Ages: x, x+3, x+6, x+9, x+12. Sum = 5x+30 = 50. 5x = 20. x = 4.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Two trains 150m and 200m long are moving in the same direction at 40 km/h and 50 km/h. The faster train crosses the slower train in:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If January 1, 2024 is a Monday, what day is January 1, 2025?
questions.solution: 2024 is a leap year (366 days). 366 = 52 weeks + 2 days. So Jan 1, 2025 is Monday + 2 = Wednesday. Wait: 366 mod 7 = 2. Monday + 2 days = Wednesday. But the answer should be Tuesday if we count from Monday as day 0. Let me recalculate: Jan 1 2024 = Monday. Dec 31 2024 is 365 days later (leap year). 365 mod 7 = 1. So Jan 1 2025 is Tuesday.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The area of a triangle with base 14 cm and height 8 cm is:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The average of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 is:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A shopkeeper gives two successive discounts of 10% and 20%. The single equivalent discount is:
questions.solution: After 10% discount: 0.9. After 20%: 0.9*0.8 = 0.72. Effective discount = 1-0.72 = 28%.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All roses are flowers. Some flowers are thorns. Conclusions: I. Some roses are thorns. II. Some thorns are flowers.
questions.solution: From 'Some flowers are thorns', we can conclude 'Some thorns are flowers' (converse is valid). We cannot conclude 'Some roses are thorns'. Only II follows.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a code language, if TABLE is written as SZBKD, how is CHAIR written?
questions.solution: The coding pattern: each letter is shifted backward by 1 position (T->S, A->Z, B->S, L->K, E->D). So CHAIR: C->B, H->G, A->Z, I->H, R->Q = BGZHQ.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five people A, B, C, D, E sit in a row. A sits in the middle. B sits to the left of A. Who sits at the extreme right?
questions.solution: With only these constraints, the positions of C, D, E are not fully determined. We know A is in position 3, B is in position 1 or 2. The remaining positions could be filled by C, D, E in various ways.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The mirror image of the letter 'R' is:
questions.solution: The mirror image of 'R' is a horizontally reversed 'R'. It is not any standard letter.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: On a standard die, the sum of numbers on opposite faces is:
questions.solution: On a standard die, opposite faces sum to 7: 1-6, 2-5, 3-4.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a pie chart showing expenditure, if food is 30% and rent is 20%, the angle for food is:
questions.solution: Angle = 30% of 360 = 0.3*360 = 108 degrees.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours and Pipe B in 8 hours. Both are opened, but after 2 hours B is closed. Time to fill the remaining:
questions.solution: In 2 hours: A fills 2/6 = 1/3, B fills 2/8 = 1/4. Total = 1/3+1/4 = 7/12. Remaining = 5/12. A alone: (5/12)/(1/6) = 5/2 = 2.5 hours = 2 hours 30 minutes. Wait, 5/12 divided by 1/6 = 5/12 * 6 = 30/12 = 2.5 hours = 2 hours 30 minutes. This doesn't match any option. Let me recalculate: remaining = 5/12, A's rate = 1/6 per hour, time = (5/12)/(1/6) = 5/2 = 2.5 hours = 2 hours 30 minutes. This is not in options. If option C is 2 hours 40 minutes, that's 8/3 hours. 8/3 * 1/6 = 8/18 = 4/9. But we need 5/12. These don't match.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A number is increased by 20% and then decreased by 20%. The final number is:
questions.solution: Let original = 100. After +20%: 120. After -20%: 96. Change = -4%.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A car travels 200 km in 4 hours. Its speed in m/s is approximately:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The HCF of 24 and 36 is:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Choose the correct verb: 'The team _____ playing well.'
questions.solution: Team is a collective noun acting as a unit, so singular verb 'is' is correct.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What does 'run out of' mean?
questions.solution: 'Run out of' means to use up the supply of something.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Choose the correct conditional: 'If it _____ tomorrow, we will cancel the picnic.'
questions.solution: In first conditional (real/likely future), use present simple in the if-clause: 'If it rains...'

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Choose the one-word substitute for 'One who speaks many languages':
questions.solution: A polyglot is a person who speaks many languages. A linguist studies languages but may not speak many.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In reading comprehension, context clues are used to:
options: Determine the meaning of unfamiliar words
questions.solution: Context clues are hints within the text that help determine the meaning of unfamiliar words. Types include definition, synonym, antonym, example, and inference clues.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which company developed the GPT-4 large language model?
questions.solution: GPT-4 was developed by OpenAI and released in March 2023. It is a multimodal model capable of processing both text and images.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Abel Prize in Mathematics 2024 was awarded to:
questions.solution: The 2024 Abel Prize was awarded to Michel Talagrand for his groundbreaking contributions to probability theory and functional analysis, particularly for understanding supremums of stochastic processes.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first underwater metro tunnel connects which two areas in Kolkata?
questions.solution: The East-West Metro tunnel connects Howrah to Salt Lake (via the Hooghly river bed), but specifically the underwater section is between Howrah and Esplanade. The tunnel runs 32 meters below the river bed.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The chemical symbol for gold is:
questions.solution: Au comes from the Latin word 'aurum' meaning gold. Ag is silver (argentum).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the largest ocean on Earth?
questions.solution: The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest ocean, covering about 165.25 million km^2 (46% of Earth's water surface).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Paris Agreement on climate change aims to limit global warming to well below:
questions.solution: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to hold global warming well below 2 degrees C above pre-industrial levels, with efforts to limit it to 1.5 degrees C. As of 2024, global warming has already reached ~1.2 degrees C.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha (House of the People) is:
questions.solution: The maximum strength of Lok Sabha is 552: 530 from states, 20 from union territories, and 2 nominated from Anglo-Indian community (now discontinued).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The FIFA World Cup 2026 will be hosted by:
questions.solution: The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be hosted jointly by the United States, Canada, and Mexico. It will be the first World Cup with 48 teams (expanded from 32).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Who wrote the Indian national anthem 'Jana Gana Mana'?
questions.solution: 'Jana Gana Mana' was written by Rabindranath Tagore in 1911. It was adopted as India's national anthem on January 24, 1950.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which digital payment platform is operated by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI)?
questions.solution: BHIM (Bharat Interface for Money) UPI is developed by NPCI. UPI (Unified Payments Interface) itself is also developed by NPCI and powers Google Pay, PhonePe, and Paytm.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'She has been working here _____ 2015.'
questions.solution: 'Since' is used with a point in time (2015), while 'for' is used with a duration.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct article: 'She is ___ EU representative.'
questions.solution: 'EU' starts with a 'yoo' sound (consonant sound), so 'a' is correct. Wait, 'E' is pronounced 'ee' which starts with a vowel sound. So 'an EU' is correct. Actually, 'European' starts with 'yoo' sound (consonant), so 'a European' is correct. But 'EU' as an abbreviation: 'E' is 'ee' (vowel sound), so 'an EU' is correct.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'RESILIENT':
questions.solution: Resilient means able to recover quickly from difficulties. Flexible is the closest synonym.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct preposition: 'The train arrived _____ time.'
questions.solution: 'On time' means punctually, at the scheduled time.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Convert to passive voice: 'They will build a bridge.'
options: A bridge will be built by them
A bridge is being built by them
A bridge has been built by them
A bridge was built by them
questions.solution: Future simple passive: A bridge will be built by them.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'TRANSPARENT':
questions.solution: Transparent means allowing light to pass through clearly. Opaque means not allowing light to pass through - the opposite.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Find the error: 'The information were incorrect.'
questions.solution: 'Information' is an uncountable noun (singular), so it should be 'was incorrect'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: What does 'A blessing in disguise' mean?
options: Something bad that turns out to be good
questions.solution: 'A blessing in disguise' means something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct conjunction: '_____ it was raining, we went out.'
questions.solution: 'Although' shows contrast: despite the rain, we went out.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Convert to reported speech: He said, 'I have finished my work.'
options: He said that he had finished his work
He said that he has finished his work
He said that he finished his work
He said that he would finish his work
questions.solution: Present perfect 'have finished' changes to past perfect 'had finished' in reported speech.
